authoringProvenance.author: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Pro — candidate authoring assistant

items.prompt: Which explanation is a complete strategy justification rather than a pattern label or complexity claim?
constraints: The task evaluates the quality of the explanation.
A complete justification must address correctness and cost.
The boundary of applicability must be explicit.

options.text: Use prefix sums because each query becomes O(1) instead of O(n); the lower per-query complexity is sufficient to justify the strategy.
Use prefix sums because repeated range queries match that pattern; cumulative storage is the conventional approach and therefore needs no separate boundary analysis.
Preprocessing is permitted; cumulative sums let subtraction isolate each static range; total cost is O(n + q) time and O(n) space; frequent updates would change the choice.

feedback.reason: A complete justification connects the decision signal to a legal mechanism, correctness invariant, full cost model, and the boundary where the choice stops applying.
feedback.details: Strategy justification is not a restatement of the selected option. The strongest explanation identifies why preprocessing is legal, what state is stored, why the range formula returns exactly the requested aggregate, how setup and query costs combine, and which workload change would break the decision. A pattern label omits the proof, while a per-query comparison hides setup and memory. The transfer rule is to make every strategy choice auditable through the chain signal → mechanism → invariant → cost → boundary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.pattern_label: The label may name a plausible family, but it does not establish legality, correctness, or cost for this contract. It becomes useful only as shorthand after the underlying reasoning is stated.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.complexity_only: This compares one phase and assumes correctness and free preprocessing. It becomes complete only after setup, space, update lifetime, and the range-recovery invariant are included.

items.prompt: An immutable API allowlist must answer a high volume of exact token checks. The response is only allow or deny, order is irrelevant, and O(n) extra memory fits. Which justification for a hash set is strongest?
constraints: Expected-time guarantees are acceptable unless stated otherwise.

options.text: Presence-only queries need stored keys; build the set in expected O(n), answer in expected O(1), use O(n) space, and reconsider if ordered output or deterministic bounds are required.
Use a hash set because lookup structures are designed for membership, usually run in constant time, and are the standard answer for repeated key queries.
Build a hash set because the complete workload is expected O(n + q), which is asymptotically better than scanning and therefore settles the choice by itself.

feedback.reason: The complete justification aligns the output primitive, retained state, expected cost, memory allowance, and guarantee boundary.
feedback.details: Presence queries require neither rank nor original positions, so a set contains sufficient information. Its lifecycle includes one expected O(n) build and q expected O(1) lookups, using O(n) additional entries. That rationale depends on acceptable expected-time semantics and an order-insensitive result; a deterministic ordered structure may be preferable under different contracts. The transfer rule is to justify a data structure by the exact operation it supports and the guarantees the workload requires.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_fast: This slogan names neither the required operation nor the assumptions behind performance. It becomes an acceptable summary only after the full state, cost, and boundary have been established elsewhere.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_linear: The build cost alone ignores query count, extra memory, expected-versus-worst-case semantics, and output ordering. A complexity class cannot erase functional constraints.

items.prompt: A sorted array is searched for a pair summing to T with pointers at both ends. Which statement supplies the decisive correctness justification?
constraints: The output asks whether a pair with sum T exists.

options.text: If the sum is below T, even the largest remaining partner cannot rescue the left endpoint, so advance left; use the symmetric argument when the sum is above T.
The array is sorted and the task concerns a pair sum, so the familiar opposite-end two-pointer pattern is sufficient justification for each movement.
Each pointer moves at most n positions and never moves backward, so the linear operation count proves that every possible pair is handled correctly.

feedback.reason: The endpoint-elimination argument explains why each irreversible pointer move preserves every possible solution.
feedback.details: Sorted order makes the current left value the smallest and the current right value the largest among remaining candidates. When their sum is below T, pairing the left value with any other remaining value cannot reach T because every other partner is no larger than the current right value; advancing left is safe. A too-large sum yields the symmetric proof for the right endpoint. The linear movement count derives time only after this invariant establishes correctness. The transfer rule is to justify every pointer move by naming the entire class of candidates it safely discards.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.two_pointers_linear: This proves an operation bound for the chosen motion, not that the motion preserves solutions. It becomes relevant after the endpoint-elimination invariant is established.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_keyword: Sorted input enables several strategies and does not by itself determine the output relation. Opposite-end movement is justified only when comparison with T eliminates an endpoint.

items.prompt: A static array uses prefix sums P where P[i] is the sum of the first i elements. Which explanation correctly justifies answering range [l, r] as P[r + 1] - P[l]?
constraints: P[k] sums the first k elements.
The array is static during queries.

options.text: Use P[r + 1] - P[l] because it is the standard prefix-sum formula; the conventional notation is enough to settle indexing and correctness.
Both prefixes contain every contribution before l, so subtraction cancels that shared part and leaves l through r; one O(n) build supports O(1) queries until updates occur.
The formula is correct because it performs one subtraction in constant time, making it faster than visiting the elements in the requested range.

feedback.reason: The cancellation invariant proves the range formula, while the lifecycle statement explains its cost and static-data boundary.
feedback.details: With a half-open prefix convention, P[k] stores the sum of indices 0 through k-1. P[r+1] and P[l] share every contribution before l, and subtracting removes exactly that shared prefix, leaving the requested inclusive range. The stored representation costs O(n) to build and remains valid only while the underlying values do not change or are maintained consistently. Constant-time subtraction is the consequence, not the proof. The transfer rule is to state the representation invariant and derive the query operation from it rather than quoting a memorized formula.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.memorized_formula: A formula without its indexing convention is vulnerable to off-by-one errors and does not explain why it works. It becomes acceptable shorthand only when the invariant is already fixed and shared.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_fast: Execution speed does not establish semantic correctness. Subtraction is useful only because the representation makes unwanted contributions cancel exactly.

items.prompt: A candidate binary-searches the minimum capacity C for which a scheduling feasibility check succeeds. Which statement is the required justification?
constraints: The capacity domain is ordered and bounded.
The task asks for the minimum feasible capacity.
Feasibility can be checked for a proposed capacity.

options.text: If C is feasible, every larger capacity is feasible; feasibility therefore has one boundary, and each check safely discards half of the capacity domain.
Capacity is an ordered integer, so midpoint checks can always move toward the minimum even without proving how feasibility behaves at larger values.
The method is correct because it runs only logarithmically many feasibility checks and keeps narrowing the numeric interval until one value remains.

feedback.reason: Binary search on an answer requires a monotonic feasibility boundary. That implication, not the numeric domain or check count, licenses half discard.
feedback.details: The search domain is possible capacity values rather than array indices. The decisive property is upward closure: once a capacity can schedule the workload, adding capacity cannot make it infeasible. Thus false values precede true values, and a feasibility result identifies which half cannot contain the minimum. The total cost is the number of checks times the cost of one check, and the justification should also state finite bounds and termination. The transfer rule is to write the one-direction implication before using binary search on any optimization parameter.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.numeric_domain: Numbers provide an order but not a monotonic predicate. This becomes sufficient only when feasibility is proven to change in one direction over that order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.log_checks: A short sequence of checks can still discard the true answer incorrectly. The logarithmic count is a cost result after the half-discard proof.

items.prompt: Order the components of a complete final strategy justification.
constraints: Ordering is scored by preserved adjacent relations.
The final component must expose a transfer boundary.

elements.text: Name the decisive requirement or constraint signal.
Explain the state or transformation the strategy uses.
State why each operation preserves or narrows the correct answer.
Derive total time, extra space, and relevant guarantee assumptions.
State the condition under which the strategy would stop applying or another candidate would win.

feedback.reason: A justification begins with why the strategy is relevant, proves how it works, derives what it costs, and ends with its applicability boundary.
feedback.details: The signal prevents a generic pattern recital. The mechanism identifies what information is maintained or created. The invariant turns operations and discards into a correctness argument. Only after the lifecycle is understood can its time, space, and guarantee type be derived accurately. The final boundary demonstrates transfer rather than overgeneralization by naming the constraint change that would invalidate or disfavor the choice. Adjacent-relation scoring reflects these dependencies rather than treating the five statements as independent buzzwords.
